title: 第 47 節
en: and victory, so that his name and that of Alfodr^ All-father, were blended together^.

Those who take this view derive Odins name not unnaturally from an old word, akin to the Latin vdtes^, a prophetic singer or bard, and compare with it the O, N. dor, inspiration. Eut they have never shown Jiow vdtes in Latin conld become Odinn in Old Norse, and W'uotan in Old High-German ^.

Grimm, in his Betitsche Mythologie, did not look upon this view of Odin as any longer requiring even a refutation. He treated the name of Wuotaii and Odin as from the beginning a name of a superhuman being. He derived the O. H. G. Wuotan, the Lombardian Wodan or Guodan, the Old Saxon Wuodan and Wodan (Westphalian Guodan and Giidan), the A. S. Woden, Frisian Weda, the Old Norse OSinn, from the O. H. G. verb tcatan, tvttot, 0. N. va^a, 6d, meaning to move along quickly, then to be furious, a transition of meaning which is likewise found in Lat. xeld and veltemens^ iMo and impetus. This root ^ Cleasby and Vigfusson, Icelandic Dictionani, s.v.

'^ Corpus Poeticum. BoreaJe, Vigfusson and Po%vell, vol. i, p. civ. The etymology of Latin vdtes is as yet unknown. The root vat in api-vat is very obscure. It occurs four times only in the Veda, and seems to me to mean no more than to go near, to obtain, in the causative to invite or to welcome. How from vdtes we could possibly arrive at Odinn and Wuotan has never been explained. Wilhelm, Le verbis deiiominaiitis, p. 14. See note to Rv. I. 165, 13.

^ Verner's Law is extremely useful to account for exceptions to Grimm's Law, and in the true sense of the old saying, exctptio prohat regulam. But Verner's Law must not be used as a mere excuse. If we could prove that the accent in vdtes was originally on the last syllable, we might accept Low German d, though hardly the High German t. But to invert this reasoning, and to postulate the accent on the final syllable of vdtes, because we wish that it should correspond to Odinn and Wnotayi, is a very dangerous proceeding. It is equally dangerous to speak of a root vat in the sense of to know. It is not used in that meaning either in the Veda or in the Avesta.

OTHER GODS OF NATURE. 323 watan, however, cannot be connected with Latin vddere, unless we take the Latin d as the representative of an original media aspirata. From this vxdan Grimm derives the substantive ivuot, wrath, fury, dv\x6s, and the 0. N. 6ffi\ mind, poetry, song ; A. S. U'od, voice, song.

As the supreme god of the Teutonic nations, Wuotan's character is summed up by Grimm in the following words ^ : ' He is the all-pervading creative and formative power, who bestows shape and beauty on man and all things, from whom proceeds the gift of song and the management of war and victory, on whom at the same time depends the fertility of the soil, nay wishing, and all the highest gifts and blessings.'

In the popular legends, however, what may be called his etymological character is still far more clearly perceptible. Wuotan is there the furious god, the god of war and victory, armed with his spear (Gungnir), and followed by two wolves {Geri and Field), two ravens (Huginn and Muninn). He also sends the storm, rides on the gale, has his waggon or wain, and his horse. In the Old Norse legends he is an old man with a broad hat and a wide mantle, heMu-maor, a hooded man, and as such he appears in the German Hakolbereitd, the leader of the wild host, whose memory lives on even now in John Hachlebirnie's house, though he is no doubt quite unconnected with Hcdieherg, i.e. Mount Hecla^.
This root, in High German watan, would presuppose ^ Translation of Grimm's German Mytlwlogy, by Stallybrass, vol. i. p. 13-2.
2 W^hy Mercury was identified with Wuotan, see M. M., Seleded Essays, i. 406 ; ii. 210 ; Grimm's Deutsche Mythologie, pp. 157-148.

a low German d, and a classical dh. As li in Sanskrit is a neutral exponent of gh, dh, bh. we should have to postulate an original vadh, for vah (part. voc?/ia, for vah-ta). In vehemens also we see traces of the same transition of meaning as in ivuot, fury.

Grohmann proposed to identif}^ Wuotan w^ith the Vedic Vat a, wdnd, and at first sight that etymology is very tempting. But vat a is known to have the accent on the first syllable, and ought therefore to show th in Low, d in High German.

Still, Grohmann was right in making Wtiotan the god of wind and weather, only that his etymon seems to me to lie not so much in the wind, as in the weather. Weathei^ before it took its general meaning meant stormy weather. This is still very clear in the German, Wetter-leuchten {iL-eier-leich, of. rikvan), Donner-icetter, Wind und Wetter, Umcetter, WetterscJdag, &c., and even in the English weatherheaten. It is the O. H. G. ivetav, A. S. iveder, O. N. veor. The th in Modern English tveather is dialectic. The same word exists in the Veda, namely, as vadh as and vadhar (Delbrlick, in K. Z. xvi. 265); but it there means the actual thunderbolt of Indra and of his enemies, and also weapon in general. From the same root we have vadha striker and weapon ; vadhatra, weapon; vadhasna, Indra's thunderbolt. In Greek this root has been preserved in w^eo), in h-o(ri-y6<^v, earthstriker, &c. (see Curtius, s. v.). From this root, and from no otlier, is derived Wuotan, literally the striker w^ith the thunderbolt, the weather-god, the storm-god \ 1 There is another word in Old Norse, 6fr ; and as Freyja is called 6ds meij (Od's maid , this can only be another name for odinn (Volusp. 87, Corp. Poet. Bar., vol. ii. p. 624 ; Hyndla's Lay, ibid., vol. ii. p. 517).

OTHEE GODS OF NATURE. 3^5 If then the name of Wuotan meant originally the weather-ffod, the wielder of the thunderbolt, we must begin with that concept, and slowly trace the transition from the furious huntsman to Odin, the Allfather, the solemn and majestic deity, just as we saw^ HiLvakan, the lord of the winds, assume the supremacy over all other gods among certain American tribes, and as in India we could watch the Maruts bec<J»ming changed into purely moral divinities, presided over by Rudra or Dyaus, as sovereign gods.
The Mixed Character of Ancient G-ods.

Besides the lesson which we have thus learnt from a comparative study of Ameiican, Babylonian, Indian, and Teutonic mythologies, as to the possible development of the highest concept of divinity out of the simplest phenomena of nature, there is another lesson which was impressed upon us when studying the histor}^ of Agni, and which is even more strongly inculcated by the history of the ^torm-gods. The ancient gods were not restricted to one character. Agni, for instance, was, no doubt, the fire on the hearth, but any poet might speak of him as born in the sky, as lightning, as rising, as the sun in the morning, and setting in the evening, as generated by ist nicht bloss typisch der in Wolkenmantel und Nebelkappe verhiillt auftrotende Gewittergeist, der als solcher audi den Beinamen DrepsvurjJi; d. h. lethalHer jaciens, "der mit dem Blitzspeer Todtende" fiihrt, und so auch geradezu zum Todesgott wird, sondern speciell auch noch in analogem Sinn der dominus larvariim et spedronwi, als welchen ihn der nordische Beiname Draugadrottin vor allem kennzeichnet, welcher ihn so recht eigentlich in der Gewitternaclit an der Spitze alles Spukes anftreten liisst/ Schwartz, Indogermanischer Volksglaube, 18&5, p. 163.
^ G-iffbrd Lectures, vol. i. p. 453.

the fire-sticks, nay, as identical with the warmth and life of the vegetable and animal world. In like manner, the father of the Maruts is not only a meteoric deity, sending his arrows from the clouds, he is also a celestial deity, he is, in fact, one side of that power of light and life w^hich is recognised in the sky, and called Dyaus. and recognised in the sun, and called S var. We distinguish between the sky, and the sun, and the morning, and the thunderstorm, and so, no doubt, did the ancient poets of the Veda. But the}^ also recognised a common element, or, if you like, a common agent behind all these phenomena of nature, and they had no difficulty in ascribing the same deeds to Agni, Dyaus, Svar or Surya, the Maruts, and the Kudras. Thus it happens that in later phases of mythology one god who has assumed a definite personality may nevertheless display some solar or celestial or meteoric characteristics w^hich cling to him from an earlier stage of his existence. Apollo, as we know him in Homer, is not the sun, but he has retained some solar qualities. Athene is not the dawn, but she has not lost all matutinal features. Zeus certainly is not simply the sky, and yet his character would be unintelligible unless we could trace him back to the Vedic Dyaus, the sky.
The Theogonic Development.

I hope it will not be supposed that, because in this course of lectures I have given such prominence to the fire and the storm-wind as powerful stimulants in the religious life of mankind, my conviction has been changed that it was the sky and the sun who OTHER GODS OF NATURE. 327 gave from the first the most powerful impulse to the growth of m^'thological and religious ideas. My only reason for passing these two theogonic processes over at present was that they have been most fully analysed before by myself and by others, and that I thought I might without presumption refer my hearers here to what I have written on this subject in my Lectures on the Science of Language, and in my Hibbert Lectures on the Origin and Growth of Religion with special reference to India. What has been called the Religion of Dyaus and the Solar Myth may be reckoned among the best secured gains of modern scholarship. Mco/xr/o-crat th ^akXov rj fjnixiireTat. The only new light which has been thrown on these theogonic processes is that we understand now how what were considered hitherto as mere facts are in reality the necessary results of our mental constitution. We know now that, like the fire and the storm-wind, the sky and the sun also could only be named by names expressive of agency. Whether we call this a necessity of language or thought, it is, as we saw, a necessity from which we cannot escape. At first these celestial, solar, igneous or meteoric agents, having become the objects of early thought, were described according to their manifold manifestations, particularly such as influenced the life and the acts of man. After a time, however, these various manifestations were recognised as external only, and the agent, being: more and more divested of these external veils, was slowly recognised as something else, something by itself, something beyond the finite knowledge of man, and in the end as something sub-natural, supernatural, and infinite. This led naturally to the two phases of 328 LECTURE XIII.

Henotheism and Polytheism, and by a still more powerful abstraction, to Monotheism, that is, the recoonition of one agent, one author, one father, one God, hidden behind the magic veil of nature, but revealed by an irresistible necessity which postulates something infinite and divine in the agents of the objective world, because it has discovered something infinite and divine in the subjective w^orld, in the agent within, or in the self.

We may thus discover in all the errors of mythology, and in what w^e call the false or pagan religions of the world, a progress towards truth, a yearning after something more than finite, a growing recognition of the Infinite, throwing off" some of its veils before our eyes, and from century to century revealing itself to us more and more in its own purity and holiness. -And thus the two concepts, that of evolution and that of revelation, which seem at first so different, become one in the end. If there is a purpose running through the ages, if nature is not blind, if there are agents, recognised at last as the agents of one Will, behind the whole phenomenal world, then the evolution of man s belief in that Supreme Will is itself the truest revelation of that Supreme Will, and must remain the adamantine foundation on which all religion rests, whether we call it natural or supernatural.
WHAT DOES IT LEAD TO? Value of Historical Studies.
I HAVE finished my survey of Physical Religion, and I feel that I ought not to shrink in my last lecture to-day from answering a question that has often been asked, namely, What does it all lead to?

You know that Lord GifFord's idea of founding lectureships on Natural Religion in the four Universities of Scotland has been criticised from different quarters, and that the lecturers also, who have endeavoured to the best of their powers to carry out the noble plan of the founder, have not escaped the strictures of unfriendly critics.

What can it all lead to? What can be the use of lectures on the origin and the history of the ancient or so-called natural relig-ions of mankind? has been the outcry of many writers, both in religious and anti-religious papers and proposals have not been wanting as to how this munificent benefaction might be employed for other and more useful purposes.
Our schools and universities have long been told that much of what they are teaching is useless in 330 LECTLJKE XIV.

the battle of life. Greek and Latin are called dead languages. Ancient history is condemned as a medley of legendary traditions and of one-sided partisan views, while our newspapers are said to contain more wisdom than the whole of Thucydides ; nay, as has lately been calculated, more words also — which is quite possible. As to philosophy, it is looked upon as obsolete, nay, even as mischievous, and the athletics of the cricketfield are praised as far more efficacious in forming manly and practical characters than the intellectual gymnastics of Logic and Metaphysics.

While every ettbrt is being made to sweep away all ancient lumber and classical rubbish, we can hardly be surprised that an attempt to introduce into our universities a new study, the study^ of dead religions, should have met with anyttiing but a friendly w^elcome on the part of educational reformers.

So far as these attacks are directed au'ainst all scientific studies which cannot at once show what they lead to, or produce useful and marketable results, no defence seems to me necessary. We surely^ know by this time how often in the history of the world the labours of the patient student, jeered at by his contemporaries as mere waste of time and money and brains, have in the end given to the world some of its most valued possessions. Faraday died a poor man, but the world has grown richer and brighter by his labours. Copernicus, while he was quietly observing, measuring, and calculating, — looked upon as a strange and even as a dangerous man by his fellow-canons at Frauenburg — never asked what all his work would lead to. Like every true student, he was simply in love with truth. And yet there, has scarcely ever been WHAT DOES IT LEAD T0 1 331 a greater revolution achieved in the world of thought, or a more important advance made in our knowledge of the universe, nay, in our knowledge of ourselves, than by that solitary philosopher in the North of Germany, when he proved that we and our globe did not form, as was fondly supposed, the centre of the universe, but had to take the place assigned to us by the side of other planets, all moving at a greater or smaller distance around one central sun.
zh: 及勝利，使得他的名字與阿爾福德（Alfodr）——全能父（All-father）——的名字混融在一起。
持此觀點者並非不合理地將奧丁（Odin）之名源自一古詞，其與拉丁文 *vates*（先知歌手或吟遊詩人）相通，並將其與古北歐語 *dor*（靈感）相比較。然而，他們從未示明拉丁文 *vates* 如何能在古北歐語中變成 *Odinn*，以及在古高地日耳曼語中變成 *Wuotan*。

格林在其《日耳曼神話學》中，不再認為這種關於奧丁的觀點有任何需要駁斥的地方。他從一開始就將烏奧塔伊（Wuotaii）和奧丁的名字視為一個超越人類的存在之名。他將中古高地日耳曼語的烏奧坦（Wuotan）、倫巴底語的沃丹或古奧丹（Wodan 或 Guodan）、古撒克遜語的烏奧丹和沃丹（西伐利亞方言 Guodan 和 Giidan）、古英語的沃登（Woden）、弗里西亞語的韋達（Weda）、古北歐語的奧辛（OSinn）追溯至中古高地日耳曼語動詞 twaetan、twetot、古北歐語 vá∂a、ó∂（意為迅速移動，後引申為狂暴），這種語義轉變也見於拉丁語的 *veld 和 *veltemens*，即動力與衝動之意。此根詞^ 見克列斯比與維格弗松《冰島語詞典》，相關條目。

' Corpus Poeticum. Boreaïe, Vigfusson and Powell, vol. i, p. civ. 拉丁文 vâtes 的語源迄今未明。api-vat 中的根 vat 極為晦澀。它在吠陀中僅出現四次，在我看來不過意味著靠近、獲得，在使役態則表示邀請或歡迎。我們究竟如何能從 vâtes 推導出 Odinn 和 Wuotan，至今未有人解釋。Wilhelm, Le verbis denominatiuis, p. 14. 見 Rv. I. 165, 13 的註解。

韋爾納定律對於解釋格林姆定律的例外情形極其有用，在古諺的真正意義上，例外證實規則（exctptio prohat regulam）。但是韋爾納定律絕不能被當作單純的藉口。如果我們能夠證明 vdtes 中的重音原本在最後一個音節上，我們或許可以接受低地德語的 d，雖然幾乎不能接受高地德語的 t。但是反轉這種推理過程，僅因為我們希望它應該與 Odinn 和 Wnotayi 相對應，就假定 vdtes 的重音在最後音節上，這是一種非常危險的做法。將根詞 vat 理解為「認識」的意義同樣危險。這種意義既不在吠陀中使用，也不在阿維斯陀中使用。
其他自然神祇。323 然而，watan 無法與拉丁文 vddere 相連，除非我們將拉丁文的 d 視為原始濁音帶氣音的代表。從此 vxdan，格林（Grimm）推導出名詞 ivuot，憤怒、狂怒、dv\x6s，以及古北歐文 6ffi，心靈、詩歌、歌唱；盎格魯-撒克遜文 U'od，聲音、歌唱。
作為日耳曼民族的至高之神，烏歐坦的性格由葛林姆用以下言詞概括^：「他是無所不在的創造與塑造之力，賦予人類與萬物以形態與美麗，歌曲之禮、戰爭與勝利之治理皆源於他，同時土地之豐饒、願景與所有最崇高的禮物和祝福都賴於他。」

在民間傳說中，然而，所謂他的**詞源學特徵**仍然清晰可見得多。烏歐坦（Wuotan）在那裡是狂暴之神、戰爭與勝利之神，手持長槍（Gungnir），並有兩匹狼（Geri and Freki）與兩隻烏鴉（Huginn and Muninn）相隨。他也發送風暴，騎乘狂風，擁有他的車隊或大車，以及他的馬匹。在古北歐傳說中，他是一位戴著寬帽、穿著寬闊披風的老人，是穆·瑪歐爾（Mu-maor），一個戴兜帽的人，正如此，他在德國哈柯爾貝萊茲德（Hakolbereitd）中現身，那野生群眾的領導者，其記憶直到現在仍在約翰·哈克爾伯尼之家中存活，雖然他無疑完全與赫德爾伯格（Hcdieherg）無關，亦即赫克拉山（Mount Hecla）。
這個詞根在高地日耳曼語中為 watan，會預設 ^ 格林《日耳曼神話》的英譯本，史塔利布拉斯譯，第一卷，第13-2頁。
2 關於墨丘利何以與烏歐坦相等同，見穆勒著《精選論文集》第一卷第406頁；第二卷第210頁；以及格林《德意志神話學》第157-148頁。
德語的低級 d 和古典的 dh。由於梵文中的 li 是 gh、dh、bh 的中性代表，我們應該為 vah（分詞 vocalia，即 vah-ta）假設原始的 vadh。在 vehemens 中，我們也看到與 ivuot「狂怒」相同的意義轉變的痕跡。
格羅曼提議將烏歐坦與吠陀的跋多（vāta）風相同定，乍看之下這個詞源學說法非常誘人。但跋多已知重音落在第一音節，因此在下德語中應當呈現 th，在高地德語中應呈現 d。

格羅曼做得沒錯，將烏奧坦視為風雨之神，只是他的詞源學解釋似乎應該更多指向天氣，而非風本身。「天氣」（weathei）在取得一般性含義之前原本特指暴風雨天氣。在德語中這點仍然清晰可見，如「閃電天氣」（Wetter-leuchten）、「雷電天氣」（Donner-wetter）、「風和天氣」（Wind und Wetter）、「變天」（Umwetter）、「暴風雨」（Wetterschlag）等詞，甚至在英語的「受風化」（weatherbeaten）一詞中亦然。其源頭為古高地德語 wetan、盎格魯撒克遜語 weder、古北歐語 veor。現代英語 weather 中的 th 是方言性的。同一詞根在吠陀中存在，即 vadhas 和 vadhar（德爾布呂克，見《比較語言學雜誌》第十六卷第 265 頁）；但在那裡它指的是因陀羅及其敵人的真實雷霆，以及武器的總稱。源自同一詞根的還有 vadha（打擊者和武器）、vadhatra（武器）、vadhasna（因陀羅的雷霆）。在希臘語中，此詞根保存在 ϝέω 和 ἀριστόφονος（地面打擊者，等等）中（參見柯蒂烏斯著，該條目）。烏奧坦即源自此詞根而非其他，字面意為用雷霆打擊者，乃天氣之神、風暴之神。¹古北歐語中另有一詞 óðr；由於芙蕾亞被稱為「奧德的侍女」（Od's maid），此詞只能是奧丁的另一個名字（《沃盧帕》第 87 節，《古北歐詩歌集》第二卷第 624 頁；《欣德拉之歌》，同上，第二卷第 517 頁）。

其他自然之神。^35 因此，若烏歐坦（Wuotan）之名原本意指氣象之神、掌雷霆者，我們必須從該概念開始，逐步追蹤其從狂暴獵人向奧丁的轉變——奧丁為眾神之父，一位莊嚴肅穆的神祇。正如我們曾看到希爾瓦坎（Hilvrakan）為風之主宰，在某些美洲民族中掌握了對所有其他諸神的至尊地位；又如在印度，我們得以觀察馬魯茨神群（Maruts）逐漸轉變為純然道德性的神祇，受到魯陀羅（Rudra）或特尤斯（Dyaus）此等主神的統轄。

當我們仔細審視古代文獻中那些被稱為「神」（gods）的存在時，很快就會發現它們絕非純粹單一的產物。這些神祇往往呈現出一種混合的性格，既帶有自然現象的痕跡，又承載著人類心靈的投射。在吠陀（Veda）的頌歌中，我們看到特尤斯（Dyaus）既是蒼穹本身，又是天父；提婆（Deva）既指閃耀者，也指神聖者。這種雙重性並非偶然，而是宗教意識發展過程中必然出現的現象。 穆勒指出，古代神祇的混合性格源於語言本身的模糊性。當原始人類試圖用有限的詞彙表達無限者（the Infinite）時，他們不可避免地將具體與抽象、自然與超自然交織在一起。這種「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）導致了神話的產生，也使得後世的研究者難以釐清古代宗教的真正面貌。因此，宗教學（the science of religion）的首要任務，便是透過比較神學（comparative theology）與比較神話學（comparative mythology）的方法，還原這些混合性格背後的本質。

除了從美洲、巴比倫、印度及日耳曼神話學的比較研究中所汲取的教訓——即最高的神聖概念如何可能從最簡樸的自然現象發展而來——還有另一個教訓，在我們研究阿格尼（Agni）的歷史時已經深刻銘心，而在研究風暴之神的歷史中更為強烈地被灌輸於我們心中。古代的神祇並非侷限於單一的性格。例如，阿格尼固然是爐火上的火，但任何詩人都可能將他描述為生於天空、作為閃電、升起、作為晨日，以及在傍晚時落下、由風摩擦木頭而生。[^1] [^1]: 德文註釋：不僅是典型的披著雲衣與霧帽出現的雷鳴之靈，他作為此類者也有著"Drepsvurji"之別名，即致人於死的閃電槍投擲者（lethaliter jaciens）之意，從而直接成為死神；而且特別亦以類似的方式是幽靈與妖魅之主（dominus larvarum et spectronwi），北方日耳曼別名Draugadrottin最為特別地將其標誌出來，此別名使其實實在在地在雷鳴之夜率領一切幽靈而出現。參見Schwartz著《印歐民間信仰》（Indogermanischer Volksglaube），1885年版，第163頁。
^ 吉福德講座，第一卷，第四五三頁。

火棍，甚至視之為與植物界和動物界的溫暖與生命相同一。同樣地，馬魯特斯神（the Maruts）的父親不僅是流星神，從雲中射出他的箭矢，他也是天體神，實際上，他是那種在天空中被識別、被稱為特尤斯（Dyaus）的光與生命之力的一個側面，也在太陽中被識別、被稱為斯瓦爾（Svar）。我們區分天空、太陽、晨曦和雷暴，古代吠陀詩人無疑也是如此。但他們也認識到一個共同的要素，或者說，如果你願意的話，一個共同的代理力量潛在於所有這些自然現象之後，他們毫不困難地將同樣的事蹟歸於阿格尼（Agni）、特尤斯（Dyaus）、斯瓦爾（Svar）或蘇里耶（Surya）、馬魯特斯神和魯德拉神（the Kudras）。因此，在神話學的後期階段中，一個已經獲得明確人格的神靈仍然可能展現某些太陽的、天體的或流星的特徵，這些特徵是從他存在的早期階段粘著而來的。阿波羅（Apollo）如我們在荷馬中所知，並非太陽，但他保留了某些太陽特質。雅典娜（Athene）並非曙光，但她並未喪失所有晨曦的特徵。宙斯（Zeus）固然並非單純的天空，然而除非我們能夠將他追溯回吠陀的特尤斯（Dyaus），即天空本身，否則他的性格將無法理解。

在宗教學的研究中，我們發現許多古代民族在其神聖概念的演進過程中，經歷了從簡至繁的發展軌跡。這種神論的發展（theogonic development）並非單純的歷史累積，而是深深植根於人類語言與思維的本質之中。 當我們考察雅利安民族的宗教傳統時，特別是透過吠陀經典的記載，便能觀察到這一發展的具體形貌。最初的信仰形態往往呈現為對自然現象的人格化；隨著時間的推移，這些人格化的自然力逐漸演變為具有更高道德與形上意涵的神祇。 在梨俱吠陀中，我們見到特尤斯（Dyaus）與其他提婆（Deva）的眾神並存，呈現出多神教（polytheism）的樣貌。然而，進入奧義書時期，對無限者（the Infinite）的沉思逐漸居於核心地位，反映出人類靈性思維的深化。 此種神論發展的過程，應當被理解為人類在面對超越性存在時，藉由語言與概念的演進而進行的精神追求。它既非單純的迷信退化，亦非直線式的進步，而是人類心靈在特定歷史與文化條件下，對終極關懷的不斷探索與重構。

我希望不會有人認為，因為在這一系列講座中我對火與風暴這兩種強大的宗教生活刺激物給予了如此突出的地位，我對於天空與太陽乃是推動人類神話與宗教觀念增長的最初、最強大力量這一信念已經改變。我之所以暫時擱置這兩個神源論過程，唯一的原因是它們已經被我自己和他人最充分地分析過了，而我認為無需妄自菲薄地向諸位提及我在《語言科學講座》以及《希伯特宗教起源與發展講座——兼及印度的專題研究》中所寫的相關內容。所謂「特尤斯宗教」與「太陽神話」（Religion of Dyaus and the Solar Myth）可謂現代學術中最為確固的成果之一。ἡ σχέσις τῆς πράξης ἢ ἡμιπρέπεται。這些神源論過程上所投射的唯一新的光芒乃是：我們現今理解了那些迄今被視為純粹事實的現象，實際上乃是我們心智構成的必然結果。我們現在知道，猶如火與風暴一樣，天空與太陽也只能以表達主動性的名稱來命名。無論我們將之稱為語言或思維的必然性，它如我們所見，乃是一種我們無法逃脫的必然性。起初，這些天體、太陽、火焰或流星現象一旦成為早期思維的對象，便被根據其種種多樣的表現，特別是那些影響人類生命與行為的表現而加以描述。然而經過一段時間之後，這些種種表現被認識為僅屬外在，而該主動者則逐漸被剝除這些外在的面紗，並緩慢地被認識為別的什麼東西——某種獨立存在的東西、超越人類有限知識之外的東西，最後則被認識為某種非自然的、超自然的與無限的東西。這自然而然地導向了兩個階段。
單一神教（henotheism）與多神教，以及透過更為強大的抽象化作用而達至一神教（monotheism），亦即對一位代理者、一位創造者、一位父親、一位上帝的認識——祂隱藏在自然的魔幻面紗之後，但卻因一種不可抗拒的必然性而被啟示出來。此種必然性要求在客觀世界的代理者身上承認某種無限且神聖的事物，因為人已在主觀世界、在內在的代理者或自我之中發現了某種無限且神聖的事物。

我們因此可以在神話的所有謬誤中，以及在我們所謂世界上的虛假或異教宗教中，發現朝向真理的進展，對超越有限之物的渴望，以及對無限者日益增長的認識——無限者在我們眼前脫去了某些面紗，並且從世紀到世紀向我們更加充分地顯示了自身的純潔與聖聖。因此，演化的概念與啟示的概念，乍看之下如此迥異，最終卻化為一體。若歷代相承存有一個目的，若自然並非盲目的，若有種種動因最終被認知為某一意志的動因，作用於整個現象世界的背後，那麼人類對那至高意志的信念的演化，本身就是那至高意志最真實的啟示，並且必然成為一切宗教所倚賴的金剛不壞之基礎，無論我們將其稱之為自然的或超自然的。

我們或許會問，這一切究竟導向何方？對宗教學的歷史研究，其最終價值何在？它是否僅僅是滿足一種學術上的好奇心，一種對人類過往錯誤與幻想的考古學式探究？或者，它能夠為我們當代的宗教與哲學問題帶來真正的啟發？ 歷史研究，尤其是對宗教起源與發展的追溯，其首要價值在於它教導我們謙卑。它向我們展示，那些我們今日視為理所當然的信仰與教義，並非憑空而降的永恆真理，而是在漫長的歷史過程中，經由無數心靈的掙扎、思考與妥協，逐步形成的。它揭示了人類宗教意識的演變軌跡——從對「無限者」（the Infinite）最模糊的感知，到對自然力量的崇拜（多神教，polytheism），再到如《梨俱吠陀》（Rig-Veda）中所見的「單一神教」（henotheism）或「輪換主神教」（kathenotheism），最終在猶太教、基督教與伊斯蘭教中達到「一神教」（monotheism）的明確形態。這個過程並非線性的進步，而是充滿了迂迴、倒退與創造性的綜合。 穆勒（Müller）曾指出，神話學的許多謎團，源於「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）。當古人用來描述自然現象（如黎明、雷電）的詞彙，其原始意義被遺忘時，這些詞彙便化身為具有人格的神祇。例如，雅利安（Aryan）語系中的「特尤斯」（Dyaus）——即希臘的宙斯與羅馬的朱庇特——最初不過是「閃耀的天空」之意。而「提婆」（Deva）一詞，則從「光明者」演變為神靈。透過比較神話學（comparative mythology）與語言科學（the science of language）的幫助，我們得以拆解這些語言的化石，還原古人真實的思維世界。 因此，歷史研究並非為了貶低宗教，而是為了更深刻地理解它。它讓我們看到，在閃語族（Semitic）與雅利安（Aryan）這兩大宗教傳統的背後，存在著共同的根源與相似的發展模式。它也讓我們注意到圖蘭語族（Turanian）的宗教形態，為人類精神的全貌補上重要的一塊拼圖。這種比較神學（comparative theology）的視野，使我們能夠超越單一傳統的侷限，以更寬廣、更同情的眼光，看待人類對超越性存在的永恆追尋。最終，它引導我們回到自身，促使我們反思：在我們自己的信仰與思想中，是否也潛藏著尚未被察覺的「語言的疾病」？我們是否也將隱喻當成了事實，將暫時的表述當成了永恆的教條？這，或許正是歷史研究最深刻的價值所在。
我已經完成了對物質宗教的考察，我覺得在今天的最後一場講座中，不應該迴避一個經常被提出的問題，那就是：這一切究竟導向何處？
諸位知道，吉福德勳爵創設蘇格蘭四所大學自然宗教講座的主張，已經遭到來自各方的批評，而那些講師們為了盡力執行創辦人的高尚計畫所做的努力，也同樣未能逃脫不友善批評者的指責。
這一切將導向何處？對人類古代或所謂自然宗教的起源與歷史進行講述，究竟有何用處？許多作者──無論來自宗教性或反宗教性的報刊與提案──都曾發出過這樣的質疑，並且不乏建議指出，這筆慷慨的捐贈應當如何被挪作其他更具實用性的目的。
我們的學校與大學長期以來一直被告知，它們所教授的許多內容在實際應用中是無用的。
生命的戰役。希臘文和拉丁文被稱為死語言。古代歷史被貶為傳奇傳統和片面黨派見解的大雜燴，而我們的報紙據說包含的智慧比整部修昔底德著作還要多；不僅如此，如最近有人計算的那樣，字數也更多——這完全有可能。至於哲學，它被視為已過時的，甚至被看作有害的，而板球場上的體育運動則被稱讚為遠比邏輯學與形上學的理性體操更有效地塑造男性氣概和實用品格的手段。
在各方齊心協力掃除一切古老遺跡與古典廢物之際，我們不應對一項新學科的引入大學而遭遇教育改革家的冷淡對待而感到驚訝——這項新學科便是對已亡宗教的研究。

凡是針對所有科學研究的攻擊，只要這些研究無法立即證明其成果，或無法產生有用且可推向市場的結果，對我來說似乎無須辯護。到了現在，我們必然已經知道，在世界歷史上，那些遭同代人嘲笑為純粹浪費時間、金錢和心血的學者們的勤奮工作，最終往往為世界帶來了其最珍貴的財富。法拉第死時家貧如洗，然而世界因他的勞績而變得更加富裕、更加光明。哥白尼在靜靜地觀測、測量和計算時——他在弗勞恩堡的同僚們眼中被視為怪人，甚至被視為危險人物——從未問過他的所有工作將導往何處。如同每一位真正的學者一樣，他只是單純地熱愛真理。然而，當這位德國北部的孤獨哲學家證明了我們及我們的地球並非如人們所欣然相信的那樣構成宇宙的中心，而是必須佔據被指派給我們的位置，與其他行星並肩而立，這些行星都以或遠或近的距離環繞著一個中心太陽運行時，世界思想中幾乎從未曾發生過更偉大的革命，也幾乎從未曾取得過比這更為重要的進展，不僅在我們對宇宙的認知上，更在我們對自身的認知上都是如此。
